q: The Government of India Act 1909 is also known as:
explanation: The Government of India Act 1909 is also known as the Morley-Minto Reforms — named after Lord Morley (Secretary of State for India) and Lord Minto (Viceroy of India). It introduced separate electorates for Muslims, expanded the Central and Provincial Legislative Councils, and gave Indians a limited role in governance for the first time.

q: Which Act introduced the system of 'separate electorates' for Muslims for the first time?
explanation: The Government of India Act 1909 (Morley-Minto Reforms) introduced separate electorates for Muslims for the first time. Only Muslims could vote for Muslim candidates in Muslim constituencies. This was a British strategy of 'divide and rule' — demanded by the Muslim League (founded 1906).

q: The Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms (1919) introduced which key feature in the provinces?
explanation: The Government of India Act 1919 introduced Diarchy (Dyarchy) in the provinces. Provincial subjects were divided into 'reserved' subjects (administered by the Governor — police, justice, land revenue) and 'transferred' subjects (administered by Indian ministers responsible to the legislature — education, health, agriculture, local government).

q: Who made the historic declaration of August 1917 promising 'gradual development of self-governing institutions' in India?
options: Edwin Montagu (Secretary of State for India)
explanation: Edwin Montagu (Secretary of State for India) made the declaration on August 20, 1917, in the House of Commons, promising 'gradual development of self-governing institutions' in India. This led to the Government of India Act 1919 (Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms).

q: The Government of India Act 1935 was described by Jawaharlal Nehru as:
options: 'A post-dated cheque on a crashing bank'
explanation: Nehru described the GoI Act 1935 as 'a new charter of slavery' — limited provincial autonomy but British control at the centre. Gandhi called the Cripps Mission (1942) 'a post-dated cheque on a crashing bank' — a different quote.

q: Which Act granted 'provincial autonomy' and abolished diarchy in the provinces?
explanation: The GoI Act 1935 granted provincial autonomy — abolished diarchy in the provinces (introduced by 1919 Act) and gave elected ministries responsible to provincial legislatures. Diarchy was instead introduced at the centre (never implemented — All-India Federation never came into being).

q: The Government of India Act 1935 proposed an 'All-India Federation' comprising British provinces and princely states. Was it implemented?
options: No — it was never implemented because the princely states refused to join
No — it was replaced by the Indian Independence Act 1947
explanation: The All-India Federation was NEVER implemented because the princely states refused to join. The princes (Chamber of Princes) refused to accede. Only the provincial part came into force (1937) — provincial autonomy and elected ministries.

q: Which institution was established by the Government of India Act 1935?
explanation: The GoI Act 1935 established the Federal Court (1937) at Delhi — predecessor of the Supreme Court of India (1950). The RBI was established in 1935 under the RBI Act 1934. The Supreme Court and Election Commission were established by the Indian Constitution (1950).

q: The Government of India Act 1919 introduced which feature at the centre?
options: Bicameral legislature (Council of State + Legislative Assembly)
explanation: The 1919 Act introduced a bicameral legislature at the centre — the Council of State (Upper House, 60 members) and the Legislative Assembly (Lower House, 145 members). It also introduced direct elections for the first time (with limited franchise — about 10%).

q: Arrange the constitutional reforms in chronological order: 1. Morley-Minto Reforms 2. Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms 3. Government of India Act 1935 4. Indian Councils Act 1861
explanation: Indian Councils Act 1861 → Morley-Minto (1909) → Montagu-Chelmsford (1919) → GoI Act 1935. The 1861 Act introduced non-official members; 1909 introduced separate electorates; 1919 introduced diarchy; 1935 granted provincial autonomy.

q: Who was the Viceroy of India when the Morley-Minto Reforms (1909) were introduced?
explanation: Lord Minto (Viceroy 1905-1910) was Viceroy when the Morley-Minto Reforms (1909) were introduced. Lord Morley was Secretary of State. Lord Curzon (1899-1905) was the previous Viceroy (partitioned Bengal 1905).

q: Who was the Viceroy of India when the Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms (1919) were introduced?
explanation: Lord Chelmsford (Viceroy 1916-1921) was Viceroy during the Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms (1919). Edwin Montagu was Secretary of State. Lord Reading (1921-1926) succeeded Chelmsford.

q: Consider the following about the Government of India Act 1919: 1. It introduced diarchy in the provinces. 2. It introduced bicameral legislature at the centre. 3. It introduced universal adult franchise. Which is/are correct?
explanation: Statements 1 and 2 are correct. Statement 3 is WRONG — the 1919 Act did NOT introduce universal adult franchise; voting was limited to about 10% of the population. Universal adult franchise came only with the Indian Constitution (1950).

q: The Government of India Act 1935 divided powers into how many lists?
explanation: The GoI Act 1935 divided powers into three lists: Federal List (defence, foreign affairs — centre), Provincial List (police, education — provinces), Concurrent List (criminal law, marriage — both, federal law prevails). This three-list system was adopted by the Indian Constitution (1950) as Union, State, and Concurrent lists.

q: Under the Government of India Act 1935, what was the franchise (voting rights) percentage of the population?
explanation: Under the GoI Act 1935, the franchise was limited to about 10% of the population — based on property, education, and tax qualifications. Universal adult franchise came only with the Indian Constitution (1950).

q: The 1937 provincial elections were held under which Act?
explanation: The 1937 provincial elections were held under the GoI Act 1935. The INC won in 7 of 11 provinces (Madras, Bombay, UP, Bihar, Orissa, NWFP, CP). The Muslim League won only in two provinces (Bengal, Punjab). The INC formed ministries — their first experience of governance.

q: Which Act introduced the system of 'communal representation' (separate electorates) for the first time?
explanation: The GoI Act 1909 (Morley-Minto) introduced communal representation (separate electorates) for Muslims for the first time. The 1919 Act extended separate electorates to Sikhs, Christians, Anglo-Indians, and Europeans. The 1935 Act further extended it to the Depressed Classes (after the Poona Pact, replaced with reserved seats).

q: Who was the Secretary of State for India who made the August 1917 declaration?
explanation: Edwin Montagu (Secretary of State 1917-1922) made the August 1917 declaration promising 'gradual development of self-governing institutions' in India. He then visited India and, with Viceroy Chelmsford, drafted the Montagu-Chelmsford Report (1918) — the basis for the GoI Act 1919.

q: The Indian Councils Act 1861 is significant for which reason?
options: It introduced non-official (Indian) members into the legislative councils for the first time
explanation: The Indian Councils Act 1861 introduced non-official (Indian) members into the legislative councils for the first time. The Governor-General's Executive Council was expanded to include 6-12 additional members (nominated, not elected). It was the first step toward Indian participation in governance.

q: The Reserve Bank of India was established in which year?
explanation: The Reserve Bank of India was established on April 1, 1935, under the RBI Act 1934. The GoI Act 1935 provided the constitutional framework. The RBI controlled currency, credit, and banking — and was nationalised in 1949.

q: Which of the following was NOT a feature of the Government of India Act 1919?
options: Diarchy in provinces (reserved and transferred subjects)
Provincial autonomy with elected ministries responsible to the legislature
explanation: Provincial autonomy was NOT a feature of the 1919 Act — it was a feature of the 1935 Act. The 1919 Act introduced diarchy (not full autonomy), bicameral legislature, and direct elections with limited franchise.

q: The Simon Commission (1927) was appointed to review which Act?
explanation: The Simon Commission was appointed in 1927 (arrived 1928) to review the GoI Act 1919 and recommend further reforms. It was boycotted by all Indian parties because it had no Indian member. Its report (1930) led to the Round Table Conferences and ultimately the GoI Act 1935.

q: Bihar's connection with the constitutional reforms was through:
options: The 1909 reforms — Bihar got separate electorates
The 1919 Act — Bihar got bicameral legislature
The 1861 Act — Bihar became a separate province
The 1937 elections — INC formed ministry in Bihar under Sri Krishna Singh
explanation: Bihar's connection was through the 1937 provincial elections (under GoI Act 1935). The INC won in Bihar and formed the provincial ministry — with Sri Krishna Singh as the first Premier (later Chief Minister) of Bihar and Anugrah Narayan Sinha as Deputy Premier. Bihar's first experience of elected self-government.

q: Which constitutional reform introduced the principle of 'dyarchy' (diarchy) for the first time?
explanation: The GoI Act 1919 (Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms) introduced dyarchy for the first time — in the provinces. Provincial subjects were divided into 'reserved' (Governor's council) and 'transferred' (Indian ministers responsible to legislature). The 1935 Act abolished diarchy in provinces and introduced it at the centre (never implemented).

q: The Indian Independence Act 1947 was based on which plan?
explanation: The Indian Independence Act 1947 was based on the Mountbatten Plan (June 3 Plan, 1947). It partitioned India into two dominions — India and Pakistan — and granted independence on August 15, 1947. The Act was passed by the British Parliament on July 18, 1947.
